---
title: "A/B Testing a Call to Action"
subtitle: "Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics"
description: "Simulating key ideas from classical frequentist statistics including bootstrap, CLT, hypothesis testing, and the peeking problem."
author: "Merna Saad"
date: 2026-04-15
categories: [Python, Statistics, A/B Testing]
image: thumbnail.jpg
aliases:
  - /project_1/hw1_questions.html
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
---

```{=html}
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/gsap.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/ScrollTrigger.min.js"></script>
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Set a consistent seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Global plot styling
plt.rcParams.update({
    'figure.facecolor': '#faf8f5',
    'axes.facecolor': '#faf8f5',
    'axes.edgecolor': '#888888',
    'axes.labelcolor': '#333333',
    'text.color': '#333333',
    'xtick.color': '#555555',
    'ytick.color': '#555555',
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.size': 11,
    'axes.titlesize': 13,
    'axes.labelsize': 11,
    'axes.spines.top': False,
    'axes.spines.right': False,
})

# Accent colors
COPPER = '#2c3e50'
SAGE = '#b8945a'
DARK = '#333333'
CREAM = '#faf8f5'
```

```{=html}
<div class="ab-story">
  <div class="ab-story-inner">

    <div class="ab-narrator">
      <div class="ab-character-wrap">
        <img src="../../assets/character-investigator.svg" alt="Investigator" class="ab-char ab-char-investigator active" id="char-investigator" />
        <img src="../../assets/character-presenter.svg" alt="Presenter" class="ab-char ab-char-presenter" id="char-presenter" />
      </div>
      <div class="ab-bubble" id="ab-bubble">
        <div class="ab-bubble-tail"></div>
        <div class="ab-bubble-content" id="ab-bubble-content">
          Welcome! I'll walk you through how A/B testing actually works.
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="ab-stage" id="ab-stage">

      <div class="ab-scene ab-scene-1 active" data-scene="1">
        <div class="ab-scene-title">The question</div>
        <div class="ab-question-grid">
          <div class="ab-cta ab-cta-a">
            <div class="ab-cta-label">Version A</div>
            <div class="ab-cta-text">"Sign up for our newsletter here!"</div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-vs">vs</div>
          <div class="ab-cta ab-cta-b">
            <div class="ab-cta-label">Version B</div>
            <div class="ab-cta-text">"Stay up to date by signing up!"</div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ab-scene-caption">Which version gets more people to sign up?</div>
      </div>

      <div class="ab-scene ab-scene-2" data-scene="2">
        <div class="ab-scene-title">Random assignment</div>
        <div class="ab-flow-wrap">
          <div class="ab-visitors-source" id="source-2">
            <div class="ab-source-label">Incoming visitors</div>
            <div class="ab-source-dots" id="source-dots"></div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-split">
            <div class="ab-split-arrow ab-split-up"></div>
            <div class="ab-split-arrow ab-split-down"></div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-buckets">
            <div class="ab-bucket ab-bucket-a">
              <div class="ab-bucket-label">Group A</div>
              <div class="ab-bucket-dots" id="bucket-a-2"></div>
            </div>
            <div class="ab-bucket ab-bucket-b">
              <div class="ab-bucket-label">Group B</div>
              <div class="ab-bucket-dots" id="bucket-b-2"></div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ab-scene-caption">Each visitor is randomly shown A or B. Fair comparison.</div>
      </div>

      <div class="ab-scene ab-scene-3" data-scene="3">
        <div class="ab-scene-title">Watch the conversions</div>
        <div class="ab-conversion-grid">
          <div class="ab-conv-panel ab-conv-a">
            <div class="ab-conv-label">Group A</div>
            <div class="ab-conv-dots" id="conv-a"></div>
            <div class="ab-conv-rate">
              <span class="ab-conv-rate-label">Sign-up rate</span>
              <span class="ab-conv-rate-value" id="rate-a">0%</span>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-conv-panel ab-conv-b">
            <div class="ab-conv-label">Group B</div>
            <div class="ab-conv-dots" id="conv-b"></div>
            <div class="ab-conv-rate">
              <span class="ab-conv-rate-label">Sign-up rate</span>
              <span class="ab-conv-rate-value" id="rate-b">0%</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ab-scene-caption">Filled dots signed up. Faded dots didn't. Watch the rates update live.</div>
      </div>

      <div class="ab-scene ab-scene-4" data-scene="4">
        <div class="ab-scene-title">Is the difference real?</div>
        <div class="ab-significance">
          <div class="ab-sig-row">
            <div class="ab-sig-label">Observed difference</div>
            <div class="ab-sig-value" id="sig-diff">...</div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-sig-distribution">
            <svg viewBox="0 0 400 120" class="ab-dist-svg" id="dist-svg">
              <defs>
                <linearGradient id="distGrad" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%">
                  <stop offset="0%" stop-color="rgba(44,62,80,0.05)" />
                  <stop offset="50%" stop-color="rgba(44,62,80,0.3)" />
                  <stop offset="100%" stop-color="rgba(44,62,80,0.05)" />
                </linearGradient>
              </defs>
              <path id="dist-curve" d="" fill="url(#distGrad)" stroke="#2c3e50" stroke-width="1.5" />
              <line x1="200" y1="20" x2="200" y2="95" stroke="#8a8a8a" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3,3" />
              <text x="200" y="110" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#8a8a8a">If there were no real difference</text>
              <line id="observed-line" x1="0" y1="20" x2="0" y2="95" stroke="#b8945a" stroke-width="2" opacity="0" />
              <text id="observed-label" x="0" y="15" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#b8945a" opacity="0" font-weight="500">Our result</text>
            </svg>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-sig-row">
            <div class="ab-sig-label">p-value</div>
            <div class="ab-sig-value" id="sig-p">...</div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ab-scene-caption">How likely is our result, if A and B were actually identical?</div>
      </div>

      <div class="ab-scene ab-scene-5" data-scene="5">
        <div class="ab-scene-title">The verdict</div>
        <div class="ab-verdict-final">
          <div class="ab-verdict-stat">
            <div class="ab-verdict-big" id="verdict-winner">A</div>
            <div class="ab-verdict-sub">is the winner</div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab-verdict-details">
            <div class="ab-verdict-detail-row">
              <span>Lift</span>
              <span id="verdict-lift">...</span>
            </div>
            <div class="ab-verdict-detail-row">
              <span>Confidence</span>
              <span id="verdict-conf">95%</span>
            </div>
            <div class="ab-verdict-detail-row">
              <span>p-value</span>
              <span id="verdict-pval">...</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ab-scene-caption" id="verdict-caption">Statistically significant. We can confidently say A beats B.</div>
      </div>

    </div>

    <div class="ab-progress-wrap">
      <div class="ab-progress-bar">
        <div class="ab-progress-fill" id="progress-fill"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="ab-progress-steps">
        <button class="ab-step-btn active" data-step="1">1. The question</button>
        <button class="ab-step-btn" data-step="2">2. Random split</button>
        <button class="ab-step-btn" data-step="3">3. Conversions</button>
        <button class="ab-step-btn" data-step="4">4. Significance</button>
        <button class="ab-step-btn" data-step="5">5. Verdict</button>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="ab-controls">
      <button class="ab-ctrl-btn" id="btn-prev">Previous</button>
      <button class="ab-ctrl-btn ab-ctrl-primary" id="btn-next">Next</button>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  if (typeof gsap === 'undefined') {
    console.warn('GSAP not loaded');
    return;
  }
  gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger);

  var narration = {
    1: "First, the question. We have two versions of a call to action. Our job is to find out which one gets more sign-ups.",
    2: "When a visitor arrives, we flip a coin. Heads they see Version A, tails they see Version B. Random assignment rules out other explanations.",
    3: "Now we wait. Some visitors sign up, others don't. Watch the sign-up rates converge toward their true values. This is the Law of Large Numbers in action.",
    4: "Version A looks like it's winning. But even identical versions would show some random difference. The p-value tells us how unusual our result really is.",
    5: "Our p-value is tiny. The difference we observed would almost never happen if A and B were truly the same. A really beats B."
  };

  var currentStep = 1;
  var maxSteps = 5;
  var activeTween = null;

  var bubble = document.getElementById('ab-bubble-content');
  var progressFill = document.getElementById('progress-fill');
  var btnNext = document.getElementById('btn-next');
  var btnPrev = document.getElementById('btn-prev');
  var stepBtns = document.querySelectorAll('.ab-step-btn');

  var piA = 0.22;
  var piB = 0.18;

  function killActive() {
    if (activeTween) {
      activeTween.kill();
      activeTween = null;
    }
    gsap.killTweensOf('.ab-tiny-dot, .ab-mini-dot, #dist-curve, #observed-line, #observed-label');
  }

  function typewriter(el, text, speed) {
    el.textContent = '';
    var obj = { count: 0 };
    gsap.to(obj, {
      count: text.length,
      duration: text.length * (speed || 0.02),
      ease: 'none',
      onUpdate: function() {
        el.textContent = text.substring(0, Math.floor(obj.count));
      }
    });
  }

  function showStep(n) {
    killActive();
    currentStep = n;
    showCharacterForScene(n);

    gsap.to('.ab-scene.active', {
      opacity: 0,
      y: -12,
      duration: 0.3,
      ease: 'power2.in',
      onComplete: function() {
        document.querySelectorAll('.ab-scene').forEach(function(s) {
          s.classList.remove('active');
          gsap.set(s, { clearProps: 'all' });
        });
        var scene = document.querySelector('.ab-scene[data-scene="' + n + '"]');
        if (scene) {
          scene.classList.add('active');
          gsap.fromTo(scene,
            { opacity: 0, y: 20 },
            { opacity: 1, y: 0, duration: 0.6, ease: 'power3.out' }
          );
        }
        runScene(n);
      }
    });

    stepBtns.forEach(function(btn) {
      var s = parseInt(btn.getAttribute('data-step'));
      btn.classList.toggle('active', s === n);
      btn.classList.toggle('done', s < n);
    });

    gsap.to(progressFill, {
      width: ((n / maxSteps) * 100) + '%',
      duration: 0.7,
      ease: 'power2.out'
    });

    btnPrev.disabled = n === 1;
    btnNext.textContent = n === maxSteps ? 'Restart' : 'Next';

    gsap.fromTo(bubble, { opacity: 0 }, { opacity: 1, duration: 0.3 });
    typewriter(bubble, narration[n], 0.025);
  }

  function runScene(n) {
    if (n === 1) runScene1();
    else if (n === 2) runScene2();
    else if (n === 3) runScene3();
    else if (n === 4) runScene4();
    else if (n === 5) runScene5();
  }

  function runScene1() {
    gsap.fromTo('.ab-cta-a',
      { scale: 0.85, opacity: 0, rotation: -3 },
      { scale: 1, opacity: 1, rotation: 0, duration: 0.8, ease: 'back.out(1.4)', delay: 0.2 }
    );
    gsap.fromTo('.ab-cta-b',
      { scale: 0.85, opacity: 0, rotation: 3 },
      { scale: 1, opacity: 1, rotation: 0, duration: 0.8, ease: 'back.out(1.4)', delay: 0.4 }
    );
    gsap.fromTo('.ab-vs',
      { scale: 0, opacity: 0 },
      { scale: 1, opacity: 1, duration: 0.5, ease: 'elastic.out(1, 0.5)', delay: 0.8 }
    );
  }

  function runScene2() {
    var source = document.getElementById('source-dots');
    var bucketA = document.getElementById('bucket-a-2');
    var bucketB = document.getElementById('bucket-b-2');
    source.innerHTML = '';
    bucketA.innerHTML = '';
    bucketB.innerHTML = '';

    var tl = gsap.timeline();
    activeTween = tl;
    var total = 16;

    for (var i = 0; i < total; i++) {
      (function(idx) {
        var dot = document.createElement('span');
        dot.className = 'ab-tiny-dot ab-tiny-neutral';
        dot.style.opacity = 0;
        source.appendChild(dot);

        tl.to(dot, { opacity: 1, scale: 1, duration: 0.3, ease: 'back.out(2)' }, idx * 0.15)
          .to(dot, {
            opacity: 0, scale: 0.3, duration: 0.3, ease: 'power2.in',
            onComplete: function() {
              var target = Math.random() < 0.5 ? bucketA : bucketB;
              var targetClass = target === bucketA ? 'ab-tiny-a' : 'ab-tiny-b';
              var newDot = document.createElement('span');
              newDot.className = 'ab-tiny-dot ' + targetClass;
              target.appendChild(newDot);
              gsap.fromTo(newDot,
                { opacity: 0, scale: 0, y: -10 },
                { opacity: 1, scale: 1, y: 0, duration: 0.4, ease: 'back.out(1.8)' }
              );
            }
          }, idx * 0.15 + 0.4);
      })(i);
    }
  }

  function runScene3() {
    var convA = document.getElementById('conv-a');
    var convB = document.getElementById('conv-b');
    var rateA = document.getElementById('rate-a');
    var rateB = document.getElementById('rate-b');
    convA.innerHTML = '';
    convB.innerHTML = '';
    rateA.textContent = '0%';
    rateB.textContent = '0%';

    var total = 100;
    var visitorsA = 0, convertsA = 0;
    var visitorsB = 0, convertsB = 0;
    var rateObjA = { val: 0 };
    var rateObjB = { val: 0 };

    var tl = gsap.timeline();
    activeTween = tl;

    for (var i = 0; i < total; i++) {
      (function(idx) {
        tl.call(function() {
          var gotA = Math.random() < piA;
          visitorsA++;
          if (gotA) convertsA++;
          var dotA = document.createElement('span');
          dotA.className = 'ab-mini-dot ' + (gotA ? 'mini-convert-a' : 'mini-miss');
          convA.appendChild(dotA);
          gsap.fromTo(dotA, { scale: 0, opacity: 0 }, { scale: 1, opacity: 1, duration: 0.2, ease: 'back.out(2)' });

          var gotB = Math.random() < piB;
          visitorsB++;
          if (gotB) convertsB++;
          var dotB = document.createElement('span');
          dotB.className = 'ab-mini-dot ' + (gotB ? 'mini-convert-b' : 'mini-miss');
          convB.appendChild(dotB);
          gsap.fromTo(dotB, { scale: 0, opacity: 0 }, { scale: 1, opacity: 1, duration: 0.2, ease: 'back.out(2)' });

          var newRateA = Math.round((convertsA / visitorsA) * 100);
          var newRateB = Math.round((convertsB / visitorsB) * 100);
          gsap.to(rateObjA, { val: newRateA, duration: 0.3, onUpdate: function() { rateA.textContent = Math.round(rateObjA.val) + '%'; } });
          gsap.to(rateObjB, { val: newRateB, duration: 0.3, onUpdate: function() { rateB.textContent = Math.round(rateObjB.val) + '%'; } });
        }, null, idx * 0.04);
      })(i);
    }
  }

  function runScene4() {
    var diff = piA - piB;
    var n = 1000;
    var se = Math.sqrt((piA * (1 - piA)) / n + (piB * (1 - piB)) / n);
    var z = diff / se;
    var p = 2 * (1 - normCdf(Math.abs(z)));

    var diffObj = { val: 0 };
    var pObj = { val: 1 };
    gsap.to(diffObj, {
      val: diff * 100, duration: 1.2, ease: 'power2.out', delay: 0.3,
      onUpdate: function() {
        document.getElementById('sig-diff').textContent = '+' + diffObj.val.toFixed(1) + ' pp';
      }
    });
    gsap.to(pObj, {
      val: p, duration: 1.5, ease: 'power2.out', delay: 0.5,
      onUpdate: function() {
        document.getElementById('sig-p').textContent = pObj.val.toFixed(4);
      }
    });

    drawDistribution();
    animateObservedLine(z);
  }

  function drawDistribution() {
    var path = document.getElementById('dist-curve');
    var points = [];
    var width = 400;
    var height = 75;
    var baseline = 95;
    for (var x = 0; x <= width; x += 4) {
      var t = (x - 200) / 40;
      var y = baseline - Math.exp(-t * t / 2) * height;
      points.push(x + ',' + y);
    }
    var d = 'M 0,' + baseline + ' L ' + points.join(' L ') + ' L ' + width + ',' + baseline + ' Z';
    path.setAttribute('d', d);
    gsap.fromTo(path,
      { opacity: 0, scaleY: 0, transformOrigin: '50% 100%' },
      { opacity: 1, scaleY: 1, duration: 1.2, ease: 'power3.out' }
    );
  }

  function animateObservedLine(z) {
    var line = document.getElementById('observed-line');
    var label = document.getElementById('observed-label');
    var targetX = 200 + z * 40;
    targetX = Math.min(Math.max(targetX, 10), 390);
    line.setAttribute('x1', 200);
    line.setAttribute('x2', 200);
    label.setAttribute('x', 200);

    gsap.to(line, {
      attr: { x1: targetX, x2: targetX },
      opacity: 1,
      duration: 1.2,
      delay: 1,
      ease: 'power3.inOut'
    });
    gsap.to(label, {
      attr: { x: targetX },
      opacity: 1,
      duration: 1.2,
      delay: 1,
      ease: 'power3.inOut'
    });
  }

  function runScene5() {
    var diff = piA - piB;
    var lift = (diff / piB) * 100;
    var n = 1000;
    var se = Math.sqrt((piA * (1 - piA)) / n + (piB * (1 - piB)) / n);
    var z = diff / se;
    var p = 2 * (1 - normCdf(Math.abs(z)));

    document.getElementById('verdict-winner').textContent = diff > 0 ? 'A' : 'B';
    document.getElementById('verdict-pval').textContent = p < 0.001 ? '< 0.001' : p.toFixed(3);

    var liftObj = { val: 0 };
    gsap.to(liftObj, {
      val: lift, duration: 1.2, ease: 'power2.out', delay: 0.4,
      onUpdate: function() {
        document.getElementById('verdict-lift').textContent = '+' + liftObj.val.toFixed(1) + '%';
      }
    });

    gsap.fromTo('.ab-verdict-stat',
      { scale: 0.7, opacity: 0, rotation: -5 },
      { scale: 1, opacity: 1, rotation: 0, duration: 0.9, ease: 'elastic.out(1, 0.6)', delay: 0.2 }
    );
    gsap.fromTo('.ab-verdict-details',
      { x: 30, opacity: 0 },
      { x: 0, opacity: 1, duration: 0.7, ease: 'power3.out', delay: 0.5 }
    );

    var cap = document.getElementById('verdict-caption');
    if (p < 0.05) {
      cap.textContent = 'Statistically significant. We can confidently say A beats B.';
      cap.className = 'ab-scene-caption ab-success';
    } else {
      cap.textContent = "Not significant. We can't rule out random chance.";
      cap.className = 'ab-scene-caption ab-neutral';
    }
  }

  function normCdf(x) {
    var a1 = 0.254829592, a2 = -0.284496736, a3 = 1.421413741;
    var a4 = -1.453152027, a5 = 1.061405429, p = 0.3275911;
    var sign = x < 0 ? -1 : 1;
    x = Math.abs(x) / Math.sqrt(2);
    var t = 1.0 / (1.0 + p * x);
    var y = 1.0 - (((((a5 * t + a4) * t) + a3) * t + a2) * t + a1) * t * Math.exp(-x * x);
    return 0.5 * (1.0 + sign * y);
  }

  gsap.to('#char-investigator', {
    y: -10,
    rotation: 2,
    duration: 3,
    ease: 'sine.inOut',
    repeat: -1,
    yoyo: true
  });
  gsap.to('#char-presenter', {
    y: -12,
    rotation: -2,
    duration: 3.4,
    ease: 'sine.inOut',
    repeat: -1,
    yoyo: true
  });

  var charactersForScene = {
    1: 'investigator',
    2: 'investigator',
    3: 'presenter',
    4: 'investigator',
    5: 'presenter'
  };

  function showCharacterForScene(n) {
    var which = charactersForScene[n] || 'investigator';
    var investigator = document.getElementById('char-investigator');
    var presenter = document.getElementById('char-presenter');
    var active = which === 'investigator' ? investigator : presenter;
    var inactive = which === 'investigator' ? presenter : investigator;

    if (active.classList.contains('active')) return;

    gsap.to(inactive, {
      opacity: 0,
      scale: 0.7,
      x: which === 'investigator' ? 30 : -30,
      duration: 0.4,
      ease: 'power2.in',
      onComplete: function() {
        inactive.classList.remove('active');
        gsap.set(inactive, { clearProps: 'x,scale' });
      }
    });

    active.classList.add('active');
    gsap.fromTo(active,
      { opacity: 0, scale: 0.7, x: which === 'investigator' ? -30 : 30 },
      { opacity: 1, scale: 1, x: 0, duration: 0.6, ease: 'back.out(1.4)', delay: 0.2 }
    );
  }

  gsap.fromTo('.ab-bubble',
    { scale: 0.9, opacity: 0, x: -10 },
    { scale: 1, opacity: 1, x: 0, duration: 0.8, ease: 'back.out(1.4)', delay: 0.3 }
  );

  btnNext.addEventListener('click', function() {
    if (currentStep === maxSteps) {
      showStep(1);
    } else {
      showStep(currentStep + 1);
    }
  });

  btnPrev.addEventListener('click', function() {
    if (currentStep > 1) showStep(currentStep - 1);
  });

  stepBtns.forEach(function(btn) {
    btn.addEventListener('click', function() {
      var s = parseInt(btn.getAttribute('data-step'));
      showStep(s);
    });
  });

  setTimeout(function() {
    showStep(1);
  }, 100);
})();
</script>
```

## Introduction

Imagine you run a website with a newsletter sign-up form on the landing page. You've been using the call to action (CTA) **"Sign up for our newsletter here!"** for a while, but a colleague suggests that **"Stay up to date by signing up!"** might resonate better with visitors. Which version actually drives more sign-ups?

Rather than relying on gut feeling, we can answer this question rigorously with an **A/B test**. The idea is straightforward: we randomly assign each incoming visitor to see one of the two CTAs (call them *CTA A* and *CTA B*) and then compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. Because assignment is random, any systematic difference in outcomes can be attributed to the CTA itself rather than to differences in who happened to see which version.

In this post, we'll walk through the statistical machinery behind an A/B test step by step. Using simulated data, we'll explore the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap resampling, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, and the connection between t-tests and linear regression. Along the way, we'll also see why "peeking" at results before an experiment is complete can lead to trouble.


## The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem

Let's formalize the setup. Each visitor to our site either signs up (which we'll code as $1$) or doesn't ($0$). We can model each visitor's outcome as a draw from a **Bernoulli distribution**:

- Visitors who see **CTA A** sign up with probability $\pi_A$.
- Visitors who see **CTA B** sign up with probability $\pi_B$.

The quantity we care about is the **difference in sign-up rates**:

$$
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
$$

If $\theta > 0$, CTA A is better; if $\theta < 0$, CTA B wins; and if $\theta = 0$, the two are equivalent.

We can't observe $\theta$ directly because we never know the true probabilities. Instead, we estimate it from data. Our estimator is the **difference in sample proportions**:

$$
\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
$$

Since each observation is either $0$ or $1$, the sample mean of a group is simply the fraction of visitors who signed up. So $\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A$ and $\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B$. The rest of this post is about understanding how well $\hat\theta$ performs as an estimate of $\theta$.


## Simulating Data

In practice, we wouldn't know the true values of $\pi_A$ and $\pi_B$. That's the whole point of running the experiment. But simulation gives us a powerful advantage: we can *set* the truth ourselves and then study whether our statistical tools recover it correctly.

Let's suppose $\pi_A = 0.22$ and $\pi_B = 0.18$, so the true difference is $\theta = 0.04$ (a 4 percentage point lift for CTA A). We'll simulate 1,000 visitors per group.

```{python}
# True parameters
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta_true = pi_A - pi_B  # 0.04

# Simulate 1,000 visitors per group
n = 1000
cta_a = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=n)
cta_b = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=n)

# Observed sample proportions
pi_hat_A = cta_a.mean()
pi_hat_B = cta_b.mean()
theta_hat = pi_hat_A - pi_hat_B

```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML

html = f"""
<div style="background: #faf8f5; border: 1px solid rgba(44,62,80,0.1); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; margin: 12px 0; border-left: 3px solid #b8945a;">
  <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: #2a2a2a; line-height: 1.8;">
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">CTA A sign-up rate:</strong> {pi_hat_A:.3f} (true: {pi_A})<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">CTA B sign-up rate:</strong> {pi_hat_B:.3f} (true: {pi_B})<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Estimated difference (θ̂):</strong> {theta_hat:.3f} (true: {theta_true})
  </div>
</div>
"""
HTML(html)
```

Our point estimate $\hat\theta$ should be in the neighborhood of the true value, $0.04$. It won't be exactly $0.04$ (that's sampling variability), but it should be reasonably close. The sections below explore *why* we can trust this estimator and how to quantify our uncertainty.


## The Law of Large Numbers

The **Law of Large Numbers** (LLN) tells us that as we collect more data, the sample mean converges to the population mean. This is the foundational reason we can trust $\hat\theta$: with enough visitors in our experiment, the difference in sample proportions will settle down near the true difference $\theta$.

To see this in action, let's compute the **running average** of the element-wise differences between our CTA A and CTA B draws. After just a handful of observations the average bounces around wildly, but as the sample grows it stabilizes near $0.04$.

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Element-wise differences
diffs = cta_a - cta_b

# Cumulative (running) average
cumulative_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

x = np.arange(1, n + 1)
# Shrinking confidence band (approximate SE around running mean)
running_se = np.sqrt(0.25 / x) * 1.96
ax.fill_between(x, theta_true - running_se, theta_true + running_se,
                color=COPPER, alpha=0.08, label='Approximate 95% band')

ax.plot(x, cumulative_avg, color=COPPER, linewidth=1.4, alpha=0.9, label='Running θ̂')
ax.axhline(y=theta_true, color=SAGE, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label=f'True θ = {theta_true}')

# Annotation arrow pointing at convergence region
ax.annotate('Estimate stabilizes\nnear the truth',
            xy=(800, theta_true), xytext=(500, theta_true + 0.08),
            fontsize=10, color=COPPER,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=COPPER, lw=1))

ax.set_xlabel('Number of Observations')
ax.set_ylabel('Running Average of θ̂')
ax.set_title('Law of Large Numbers: Convergence of θ̂ to True θ')
ax.legend(frameon=False, loc='lower right')
ax.set_xlim(1, n)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaway" appearance="simple"}
The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that our estimate converges to the truth with enough data. This is why sample size matters in A/B testing.
:::

The plot tells a clear story. Early on, with only a few dozen observations, the running average swings erratically. Sometimes it's negative, sometimes it's well above the truth. But as the sample size climbs into the hundreds and thousands, the estimate locks in near $0.04$. This is the LLN at work: more data means a more reliable estimate. It's the reason A/B tests need sufficient sample sizes before we draw conclusions.


## Bootstrap Standard Errors

Knowing that $\hat\theta$ is a good estimator is only half the story. We also need to know **how precise** it is. Is our estimate of $0.04$ tightly nailed down, or could the true value plausibly be $0.10$ or even $-0.02$?

The **bootstrap** is an elegant resampling technique that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without deriving a formula by hand. The recipe is simple:

1. Resample (with replacement) from each group, creating a new dataset of the same size.
2. Compute $\hat\theta^*$ on the resampled data.
3. Repeat many times. The standard deviation of all the $\hat\theta^*$ values is our **bootstrap standard error**.

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Bootstrap
n_boot = 1000
boot_thetas = np.empty(n_boot)

for i in range(n_boot):
    boot_a = np.random.choice(cta_a, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_b = np.random.choice(cta_b, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_thetas[i] = boot_a.mean() - boot_b.mean()

boot_se = boot_thetas.std()

# Analytical standard error for comparison
se_analytical = np.sqrt(
    pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A) / n +
    pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B) / n
)

# 95% confidence interval using the bootstrap SE
ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.hist(boot_thetas, bins=35, color=COPPER, alpha=0.75, edgecolor='white', density=True)
ax.axvspan(ci_lower, ci_upper, color=SAGE, alpha=0.18, label='95% CI')
ax.axvline(ci_lower, color=SAGE, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.3)
ax.axvline(ci_upper, color=SAGE, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.3)
ax.axvline(theta_hat, color='#2a2a2a', linestyle='-', linewidth=1.5, label=f'Point estimate θ̂ = {theta_hat:.3f}')
ax.set_xlabel('Bootstrap θ̂*')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.set_title('Bootstrap Distribution of θ̂ with 95% Confidence Interval')
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
html = f"""
<div style="background: #faf8f5; border: 1px solid rgba(44,62,80,0.1); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; margin: 12px 0; border-left: 3px solid #b8945a;">
  <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: #2a2a2a; line-height: 1.8;">
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Bootstrap SE:</strong> {boot_se:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Analytical SE:</strong> {se_analytical:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Point estimate θ̂:</strong> {theta_hat:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">95% CI:</strong> [{ci_lower:.4f}, {ci_upper:.4f}]
  </div>
</div>
"""
HTML(html)
```

The bootstrap and analytical standard errors are reassuringly close. They should agree to within a few thousandths, which confirms that the bootstrap is doing its job. The 95% confidence interval gives us a range of plausible values for $\theta$: if the interval excludes zero, we have evidence that the two CTAs perform differently.

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaway" appearance="simple"}
The bootstrap turns resampling into a practical way to quantify uncertainty without deriving a formula by hand, and the resulting standard error closely matches the analytical one.
:::


## The Central Limit Theorem

The **Central Limit Theorem** (CLT) is one of the most powerful results in statistics. It tells us that regardless of the shape of the underlying distribution, the sampling distribution of a sample mean (or a difference in sample means) approaches a **Normal distribution** as the sample size grows.

This matters for our A/B test because it means we can use the Normal distribution to make probability statements about $\hat\theta$, even though the raw data are binary (0s and 1s), not bell shaped at all.

Let's see the CLT in action. For each of four sample sizes ($n = 25, 50, 100, 500$), we'll repeat the experiment 1,000 times and plot the distribution of $\hat\theta$.

```{python}
#| echo: true
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_sims = 1000
clt_results = {}

for sz in sample_sizes:
    estimates = np.empty(n_sims)
    for i in range(n_sims):
        a = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=sz)
        b = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=sz)
        estimates[i] = a.mean() - b.mean()
    clt_results[sz] = estimates
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(9, 6), sharex=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

for idx, sz in enumerate(sample_sizes):
    ax = axes[idx]
    data = clt_results[sz]
    ax.hist(data, bins=30, color=COPPER, alpha=0.7, edgecolor='white', density=True)

    # Overlay Normal curve
    mu = data.mean()
    sigma = data.std()
    x_curve = np.linspace(data.min(), data.max(), 200)
    ax.plot(x_curve, stats.norm.pdf(x_curve, mu, sigma),
            color=SAGE, linewidth=2, label='Normal fit')

    ax.axvline(theta_true, color='#2a2a2a', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.3, label=f'True θ = {theta_true}')
    ax.set_title(f'n = {sz}', fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_ylabel('Density' if idx % 2 == 0 else '')
    ax.set_xlabel('θ̂' if idx >= 2 else '')
    if idx == 0:
        ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=8, loc='upper right')

fig.suptitle('Central Limit Theorem: Sampling Distribution of θ̂', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

At $n = 25$, the histogram looks jagged and somewhat irregular. The discrete nature of the Bernoulli data is still showing through. By $n = 50$ and $n = 100$, the distribution is smoothing out and becoming more symmetric. At $n = 500$, it's a convincing bell curve centered near $\theta = 0.04$. This progression is the Central Limit Theorem doing exactly what it promises: no matter how non-Normal the underlying data, the distribution of the estimator becomes approximately Normal with enough observations.

Notice also that the histograms become narrower as $n$ increases. This is the variance shrinking with sample size, the same intuition behind the Law of Large Numbers, now visualized as a tighter bell curve.

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaway" appearance="simple"}
Even though the raw data are binary, the Central Limit Theorem makes the sampling distribution of θ̂ approximately Normal, which is why we can use Normal-based tools for inference.
:::


## Hypothesis Testing

Now that we've established that $\hat\theta$ is approximately Normal for large samples, we can perform a formal **hypothesis test**. The question: is the observed difference in sign-up rates large enough to conclude that CTA A truly outperforms CTA B, or could it just be noise?

We set up our hypotheses as:

- $H_0: \theta = 0$ (the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate, our null hypothesis)
- $H_1: \theta \neq 0$ (the CTAs differ, our alternative hypothesis)

To test $H_0$, we **standardize** our estimate to form a test statistic:

$$
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
$$

Why standardize? Here's the logic, step by step:

1. The CLT tells us $\hat\theta$ is approximately Normal for large samples. Under $H_0$, its mean is $0$.
2. Dividing a Normal random variable by its standard deviation yields a **standard Normal**, $N(0,1)$.
3. So under $H_0$, $z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)$. We now know the *entire distribution* of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a **p-value**: the probability of seeing a result at least as extreme as ours if $H_0$ were true.

A brief note on terminology: you'll often see this called a "t-test." Strictly speaking, the t-distribution arises when data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated. Our data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so the CLT gives us approximate Normality and we're really performing something closer to a z-test. For large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction rarely matters in practice. Still, it's important to understand the reasoning behind it.

The classical t-test applies when the data come from a Normal distribution and the variance is unknown. In that setting, the ratio of the estimate to its estimated standard error follows an exact t-distribution. In our case, the data are Bernoulli, not Normal. The CLT gives us *approximate* Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. For large samples, the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence.

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Compute the z-statistic and p-value
z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytical
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))  # two-sided test
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
decision = ("Reject H₀: statistically significant difference between the two CTAs."
            if p_value < 0.05
            else "Fail to reject H₀: observed difference is not statistically significant.")

html = f"""
<div style="background: #faf8f5; border: 1px solid rgba(44,62,80,0.1); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; margin: 12px 0; border-left: 3px solid #b8945a;">
  <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: #2a2a2a; line-height: 1.8;">
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">θ̂:</strong> {theta_hat:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">SE(θ̂):</strong> {se_analytical:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">z-statistic:</strong> {z_stat:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">p-value:</strong> {p_value:.4f}<br>
    <strong style="color: #b8945a;">Decision at α = 0.05:</strong> {decision}
  </div>
</div>
"""
HTML(html)
```

The p-value tells us how likely we'd be to observe a difference as large as $\hat\theta$ (or larger) if the two CTAs were truly identical. A small p-value (below our threshold of $0.05$) means the data are hard to reconcile with the null hypothesis, and we reject $H_0$ in favor of the conclusion that the CTAs differ. A larger p-value means the difference we observed could plausibly have arisen by chance alone.

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaway" appearance="simple"}
Standardizing θ̂ by its standard error produces a test statistic whose distribution under H₀ is known, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.
:::


## The T-Test as a Regression

Here's an insight that is both elegant and practically useful: the two-sample test we just ran is **mathematically equivalent** to a simple linear regression. This connection matters because regression generalizes effortlessly. You can add control variables, interaction terms, or additional treatment arms using the same framework.

To see this, we stack all 2,000 observations into a single dataset with two columns:

- $Y_i$: the outcome ($1$ if sign-up, $0$ if not)
- $D_i$: the treatment indicator ($1$ if the visitor saw CTA A, $0$ if CTA B)

Then we fit the regression $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i$. The coefficients have a clean interpretation:

1. $\beta_0$ is the expected sign-up rate when $D_i = 0$, which is the mean of the B group, estimating $\pi_B$.
2. $\beta_0 + \beta_1$ is the expected sign-up rate when $D_i = 1$, which is the mean of the A group, estimating $\pi_A$.
3. Therefore $\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta$, and the OLS estimate $\hat\beta_1$ is **numerically identical** to $\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B$.

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Stack data into a regression-friendly format
Y = np.concatenate([cta_a, cta_b])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])

# Fit OLS regression
X_reg = sm.add_constant(D)
model = sm.OLS(Y, X_reg).fit()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
coef = model.params[1]
reg_se = model.bse[1]
reg_t = model.tvalues[1]
reg_p = model.pvalues[1]

html = f"""
<div style="background: #faf8f5; border: 1px solid rgba(44,62,80,0.1); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; margin: 12px 0; border-left: 3px solid #b8945a;">
  <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: #2a2a2a; line-height: 1.7;">
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Regression:</strong> Y = β₀ + β₁·D + ε<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">β̂₁:</strong> {coef:.4f} &nbsp;·&nbsp;
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">SE:</strong> {reg_se:.4f} &nbsp;·&nbsp;
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">t:</strong> {reg_t:.4f} &nbsp;·&nbsp;
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">p:</strong> {reg_p:.4f}
  </div>
  <table style="width: 100%; margin-top: 10px; font-size: 0.82rem; border-collapse: collapse;">
    <thead>
      <tr style="background: #2c3e50; color: #faf8f5;">
        <th style="padding: 6px 10px; text-align: left;">Quantity</th>
        <th style="padding: 6px 10px; text-align: right;">Two-Sample</th>
        <th style="padding: 6px 10px; text-align: right;">Regression</th>
      </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td style="padding: 5px 10px;">Estimate</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{theta_hat:.4f}</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{coef:.4f}</td></tr>
      <tr style="background: #f5efe6;"><td style="padding: 5px 10px;">SE</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{se_analytical:.4f}</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{reg_se:.4f}</td></tr>
      <tr><td style="padding: 5px 10px;">Test stat</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{z_stat:.4f}</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{reg_t:.4f}</td></tr>
      <tr style="background: #f5efe6;"><td style="padding: 5px 10px;">p-value</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{p_value:.4f}</td><td style="padding: 5px 10px; text-align: right;">{reg_p:.4f}</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
</div>
"""
HTML(html)
```

The point estimate $\hat\beta_1$ matches $\hat\theta$ exactly, as expected. The standard errors and test statistics may differ very slightly because OLS uses a single pooled variance estimate while the two-sample formula allows separate variances for each group. For our sample size, the difference is negligible.

The takeaway: regression and the two-sample test are two views of the same coin. When you need something more than a simple A/B comparison (say you want to control for visitor demographics or test three CTAs at once), the regression framework extends naturally, while the two-sample formula does not.

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaway" appearance="simple"}
A two-sample t-test is just a special case of simple linear regression. The regression framework scales effortlessly to controls and multi-arm tests.
:::


## The Problem with Peeking

Your boss is impatient. Instead of waiting for the full 1,000 visitors per group, she wants to check the results after every 100 visitors, peeking at the data 10 times over the course of the experiment. If *any* peek shows a significant result at $\alpha = 0.05$, she wants to declare a winner immediately.

This sounds reasonable on the surface. But it's a trap.

A single hypothesis test at the $\alpha = 0.05$ level has a 5% chance of a **false positive**, meaning we reject $H_0$ when there's actually no difference. But each additional peek is another roll of the dice. With 10 peeks, the probability that *at least one* of them triggers a false alarm is much higher than 5%.

Let's demonstrate this with a simulation. We'll set up a world where **the null is true** ($\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20$, no difference) and simulate 10,000 experiments, each with 10 peeks.

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Peeking simulation: both groups have the SAME conversion rate
pi_null = 0.20
n_experiments = 10_000
peek_points = np.arange(100, 1001, 100)  # peek at 100, 200, ..., 1000

false_positive_count = 0

for _ in range(n_experiments):
    # Simulate full experiment (1000 per group)
    a_full = np.random.binomial(1, pi_null, size=1000)
    b_full = np.random.binomial(1, pi_null, size=1000)
    
    any_significant = False
    for peek_n in peek_points:
        a_peek = a_full[:peek_n]
        b_peek = b_full[:peek_n]
        
        p_a = a_peek.mean()
        p_b = b_peek.mean()
        diff = p_a - p_b
        
        se = np.sqrt(p_a * (1 - p_a) / peek_n + p_b * (1 - p_b) / peek_n)
        if se > 0:
            z = diff / se
            p_val = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z)))
            if p_val < 0.05:
                any_significant = True
                break
    
    if any_significant:
        false_positive_count += 1

empirical_fpr = false_positive_count / n_experiments
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
labels = ['Single test\n(no peeking)', 'With 10 peeks']
values = [0.05, empirical_fpr]
bars = ax.bar(labels, values, color=[SAGE, COPPER], width=0.55, edgecolor='white')
ax.axhline(0.05, color='#2a2a2a', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Nominal 5% rate')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=0))
ax.set_ylabel('False Positive Rate')
ax.set_title('Peeking Inflates the False Positive Rate')
ax.set_ylim(0, max(values) * 1.35)
for bar, v in zip(bars, values):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, v + 0.005,
            f'{v:.1%}', ha='center', fontsize=11, color='#2a2a2a', fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(frameon=False, loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
html = f"""
<div style="background: #faf8f5; border: 1px solid rgba(44,62,80,0.1); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; margin: 12px 0; border-left: 3px solid #b8945a;">
  <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: #2a2a2a; line-height: 1.8;">
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">True state of the world:</strong> θ = 0 (no real difference)<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Experiments simulated:</strong> {n_experiments:,}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Peeks per experiment:</strong> {len(peek_points)}<br>
    <strong style="color: #2c3e50;">Nominal false positive rate:</strong> 5.0%<br>
    <strong style="color: #b8945a;">Empirical false positive rate with peeking:</strong> {empirical_fpr:.1%} ({empirical_fpr / 0.05:.1f}× the nominal rate)
  </div>
</div>
"""
HTML(html)
```

The result is striking. Instead of the expected 5% false positive rate, peeking pushes it up substantially, often to around 15 to 20% or higher. That means roughly 1 in 5 experiments would lead your boss to declare a "winner" even when neither CTA is actually better.

This is known as the **multiple comparisons problem**. Each peek is an independent opportunity for random noise to cross the significance threshold. The more you look, the more likely you are to find something that isn't there. In practice, this is why A/B testing platforms use methods like **sequential testing** or **alpha spending functions** that adjust for repeated looks at the data, keeping the overall false positive rate under control.

The lesson is simple: **decide your sample size in advance and don't peek.** Or, if you must monitor results during an experiment, use a statistical framework designed for sequential analysis.

```{=html}
<div style="background: #2c3e50; color: #faf8f5; border-radius: 10px; padding: 24px; margin: 24px 0;">
  <h3 style="color: #b8945a; font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; margin-top: 0;">What We Learned</h3>
  <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 16px; font-size: 0.85rem;">
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">LLN:</strong> More data = better estimates</div>
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">Bootstrap:</strong> Resampling estimates uncertainty</div>
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">CLT:</strong> Sampling distributions become Normal</div>
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">Hypothesis Test:</strong> Standardize to get a p-value</div>
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">Regression:</strong> T-test = simple linear regression</div>
    <div><strong style="color: #b8945a;">Peeking:</strong> Multiple tests inflate false positives</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<div class="progress-bar" id="progress-bar"></div>

<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  var progressBar = document.getElementById('progress-bar');
  if (progressBar) {
    window.addEventListener('scroll', function() {
      var scrollTop = window.scrollY;
      var docHeight = document.documentElement.scrollHeight - window.innerHeight;
      var scrollPercent = (scrollTop / docHeight) * 100;
      progressBar.style.width = scrollPercent + '%';
    });
  }

  var observer = new IntersectionObserver(function(entries) {
    entries.forEach(function(entry) {
      if (entry.isIntersecting) {
        entry.target.classList.add('visible');
      }
    });
  }, { threshold: 0.08, rootMargin: '0px 0px -40px 0px' });

  document.querySelectorAll('.level2').forEach(function(el) {
    el.classList.add('scroll-reveal');
    observer.observe(el);
  });

  document.querySelectorAll('.cell, .sourceCode').forEach(function(el) {
    el.classList.add('scroll-reveal', 'reveal-code');
    observer.observe(el);
  });

  document.querySelectorAll('.cell-output-display').forEach(function(el) {
    el.classList.add('scroll-reveal', 'reveal-chart');
    observer.observe(el);
  });

  document.querySelectorAll('.cell-output-stdout pre').forEach(function(el) {
    el.classList.add('scroll-reveal', 'reveal-output');
    observer.observe(el);
  });

  var tocLinks = document.querySelectorAll('#TOC a');
  tocLinks.forEach(function(link) {
    link.style.transition = 'color 0.3s ease, padding-left 0.3s ease';
  });
});
</script>
```
